mappings.rationale: Directly addresses the scientific study of language including phonetics, grammar, semantics, and linguistic theory (outline F).
Explores the diversity of world languages, phonology, language classification, and typology (outline C and H).
Examines language and society including dialects, language as a political instrument, and language in cross-cultural relations (outline G).
Traces the history, dialects, and standardization of a major Indo-European Germanic language (outline H.1.f).
Addresses multilingual societies, endangered languages, and pidgins and creoles (outline C.3, G.3, and H.22).
Investigates language variants including slang, jargon, and specialized registers (outline C.3).
Explores cross-linguistic communication including machine translation and sign language (outline G.3 and B.4).
Traces the origin and evolution of writing systems including cuneiform, hieroglyphic, alphabetic, and Mayan scripts (outline E).
Egyptian hieroglyphic writing is examined in detail, relevant to outline E.3 on systems of writing.
The visual representation of written language -- fonts, letterforms, and printing -- connects to outline E.4.
Explores literacy, the history of reading, and the relationship between written and spoken language (outline C.6 and E).
Lexicography, the codification of language, and linguistic authority are central to outline F.6 and C.5.
Meaning and style in language, persuasive speech, and language as a political instrument connect to outline C.5 and G.4.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
mappings.rationale: Directly addresses semantics, word meaning, speech acts, and the relationship between language and conceptualization (outline C.5 and F.5).
Interpretation theory and the relationship between language and meaning inform outline C.5 and A.
Psychological bases of speech and language processing are relevant to outline C.4 and D.2.
Reading disorders and developmental language issues are relevant to speech disorders (outline D.3).
Language acquisition and development in children illuminate the psychological bases of speech (outline C.4 and C.6).
Codes, ciphers, and encrypting language directly match outline C.8 on cryptology.
AI and human-computer interfaces are relevant to cybernetic communication (outline B.4).
Computer languages and algorithms are relevant to cybernetic communication (outline B.4).
Communication theory, signals, signs, and information systems are foundational to outline A and B.3.
Poststructuralist theories of language challenge established models of meaning and representation (outline F.1).
Theories of language in literary study -- including speech acts and poetics -- are relevant to linguistic theory (outline F.1 and C.5).
The Chinese literary tradition illuminates Sino-Tibetan languages and East Asian writing systems (outline H.6.a and E.3).
The Japanese literary tradition is relevant to the Japanese language section (outline H.13).
